Subject: Business Proposal (Mr. Paul Maroga South Africa)
Body:
From Office of the Director,
Department of Minerals and Energy
Project Implementation Unit, Department of Minerals and Energy,
Trevenna Campus, Building 2C, Cnr Meintjes & Francis Baard Streets, Sunnyside, Pretori
Republic of South Africa.

Attention:

Mineral Resources and Energy welcomes the appointment of Jacob Mbele as Director General

Dear Friend,

Having consulted with my colleagues Mr. June Williams and based on the information gathered from the Chambers Of Commerce and Industry, I have the privilege to request your assistance to transfer the sum of $47,500,000.00 (Forty-Seven Million, Five Hundred thousand United States dollars) into your accounts. The above sum resulted from an over-invoiced contract, executed, commissioned and paid for about five years (5) ago by a foreign contractor. This action was however intentional and since then the fund has been in a suspense account at The Apex Bank, South African Reserve Bank awaiting final payment to any reliable foreign bank account you may provide.

We are now ready to transfer the fund overseas and that is where you come in. It is important to inform you that as civil servants, we are forbidden to operate a foreign account; that is why we require your assistance. The total sum will be shared as follows: 70% for us, 25% for you and 5% for local and international expenses incidental to the transfer.

The transfer is risk free on both sides. My colleague Mr. June Williams is an accountant with the Department of minerals and energy company LTD, republic of South Africa. If you find this proposal acceptable, we shall require the following documents:

(a) Your banker’s name, Address, telephone, account and fax numbers.

(b) Your private telephone and fax numbers for confidentiality and easy communication.

(c) Your letter-headed paper stamped and signed.

Alternatively, we will furnish you with the text of what to type into your letter-headed paper, along with a breakdown explaining, comprehensively what we require of you. The business will take us thirty (30) working days to accomplish.

Thank you, I am looking forward to hearing from you.

My best regards,

Mr. Paul Maroga

Subject: Re: Mercury Language - Question about Asynchronous Processing
Body:
Dear John,

Thank you for providing further details. We understand your requirement for asynchronous processing to improve the performance of your application.

We are currently developing a new set of features in Mercury Language that will directly address efficient asynchronous API handling. These features are expected to be rolled out in the next few weeks.

In the meantime, while not ideal, you could potentially achieve a similar outcome using multi-threading within your existing codebase. However, this approach might require more complex error handling and synchronization on your end.

We will keep you updated on the progress of the new asynchronous features in Mercury. Please let us know if you have any further questions.

Best regards,
The Aetheros Support Team

Subject: Can the federal government restore public faith?
Body: First Up Plus, Canadian households in huge debt and a rise in pregnant people using cannabis ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ First Up By Manuela Vega Good morning. Here’s the latest on reports of foreign interference in elections, Canadian household debt and cannabis use during pregnancy. DON’T MISS Canadian Press/Sean Kilpatrick federal politics David Johnston has rejected a public inquiry and accused Canada’s spies of mismanaging foreign interference information Although a public inquiry into foreign elections meddling would have been an “easy” option, it wouldn’t lead to a full understanding of Chinese foreign interference since classified intelligence must stay secret, special rapporteur David Johnston says in a report. An inquiry would actually delay the action needed to close the gaps and duplicate what’s already been done. It also wouldn’t allow for a judicial commission to publicly share more than he could. Stephanie Levitz and Tonda MacCharles report on why Johnston will, instead, hold public hearings to produce another report in October that he says is needed to restore public trust. More: Johnston said media leaks on foreign interference were incomplete. Here’s what he found. Meanwhile: Pierre Poilievre is calling on Johnston to resign and is refusing to review classified information on foreign state interference. Susan Delacourt’s take: The much-awaited report on foreign meddling in Canadian elections rests on a bold idea: that the institutions of our democracy are capable of restoring faith in the system. Has Johnston seen politics lately? Canadian Press File Photo economy Canadian households now have the worst debt ratio of any G7 country As household debt decreased in the U.S., U.K. and Germany, and remained nearly unchanged in Italy, it rose “inexorably” in Canada, according to a report by the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation. Behind about three-quarters of that debt? Mortgages and high interest rates straining household finances, the report’s author writes. Jeremy Nuttall reports on why debt levels will continue to grow unless housing unaffordability is addressed — and how young families are the most affected. By the numbers: “At the time of the recession in 2008, (household debt) stood at about 80 per cent of the size of the economy, in 2010 it rose to 95 per cent, and by 2021 debt exceeded its size,” the report reads. It adds that at the end of 2021, Canada’s household debt-to-GDP ratio reached 107 per cent. Interactive: Use our tool to see how home prices have changed in your Toronto neighbourhood over the last decade. Canadian Press/Tijana Martin health The number of pregnant Ontarians requiring hospital treatment for cannabis use has almost doubled since 2018 A new study has found the legalization of cannabis has been associated with an increase over time in hospital visits for cannabis use during pregnancy, Megan Ogilvie writes. The authors of the report, published in the Canadian Medical Association Journal, say their conclusion suggests more targeted public health messaging is needed. Here’s what we know about the increase in the “rare but severe events” — and the associated risks. By the numbers: The absolute increase in those needing hospital care over the study period was small, the researchers stress, rising from 11 per 100,000 pregnancies to 20 per 100,000 pregnancies. Why it matters: Babies of people who received hospital care for cannabis use were more likely to be born preterm and require admission to the neonatal intensive care unit, the study found. More: Although the Society of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists of Canada recommends people avoid cannabis during pregnancy, its use during pregnancy has become more common. One 2019 U.S. study found about seven per cent of pregnant women used cannabis in 2017 — up from 3.4 per cent in 2003. Need a trusty guide through the housing crunch? Living Here, the Star’s free newsletter, can help. Each week, it’ll bring you the analysis and expert advice you need to navigate renting, buying, owning or just getting by in and around Toronto — plus more of the Star’s essential real estate coverage. Sign up for free here. WHAT ELSE “Bring it on.” Here’s how Doug Ford reacted to Bonnie Crombie’s exploratory bid for Liberal leader. Parents were “blindsided” by Peel school board’s decision to strip the name of a Black activist from new a centre. These mayoral candidates’ photos with uniformed Toronto cops has been slammed as “improper.” Which candidates for mayor are actually offering real talk on Toronto taxes? Too few this far into the race. The Peel school board is investigating after an anonymous letter alleged “violence, fear” at a Mississauga middle school. An Ontario jail guard is guilty of sexually assaulting a prisoner. Here’s why his account of events was “completely unbelievable.” Was the “incel” killer a terrorist? A court hears landmark arguments in the Toronto spa murder. Is AI coming for your job? These are the workers who will be replaced first, according to experts. Verified travellers will be able to pass through security faster at major Canadian airports — bypassing these screening steps as they go through checkpoints. Actors have spoken out about their distaste for it — has the onscreen sex scene gasped its last climactic breath? Toronto is getting a Pink Floyd exhibit, “an audio-visual sensorial journey unlike any other.” This bike locking ring with a bad lean has pedestrians bent out of shape. ICYMI Ella Olsson/Unsplash Families are having meals catered instead of buying groceries. We crunched the numbers and spoke with experts to find out if it’s really cheaper. CLOSE-UP Steven Shpak Photography DON RIVER: A mink, named “Eddie” by Toronto photographer Steven Shpak, emerges “from a snow-covered log, making his way over to his frozen pond somewhere in the Don Valley." Eddie’s picture is just one of many bringing joy to thousands of people online. Take a look at Shpak’s charming photos of fox, eagles, deer and owls. Thank you for reading. You can reach me and the First Up team at firstup@thestar.ca. I’ll see you back here tomorrow. Subject: Looking to a better future
Body: Plus: A satirical debut, and the story of the physicist who invented the atomic bomb ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ Books By Deborah Dundas After a week where climate change was top of mind with thick wildfire smoke blanketing the GTA, it makes sense to look to the future — how human nature might shape it, and how we can work together to make it better. In “Our Tribal Future,” University of Toronto biological anthropologist David R. Samson examines how we are naturally inclined to form groups. In a Q&A with the Star, Samson argues that “maladaptive tribalism” leads to negative outcomes like xenophobia and the spread of conspiracy theories. But tribalism can also be a force for good, bringing co-operation and altruism. And he should know. He is “is one of only a handful of scientists who have done field research among both human forager societies and wild chimpanzees” and grew up in a fundamentalist cult in Indiana, our interviewer, Brian Bethune, points out. “From the beginning, the tribal drive has been bug and feature, curse and blessing,” Samson told the Star. “Bringing it to people’s conscious awareness, I believe, is the only shot our species has of reducing intergroup conflict in the future.” There is more book news and reviews below, too, to get you started on a week of reading. Join us again next Friday for more from the world of books. Creating a better future: How to take our instinct to belong to a group and turn it into a force for good In his new book ‘Our Tribal Future,’ David R. Samson studies how our instincts have allowed us to co-operate, fed into xenophobia, and how we can make the world a better place Read more » When your Girlfriend’s on Mars, a tragi-comic future seems inevitable Read more » Ambition, power a love story, and the physicist who invented the atomic bomb Read more » The bestselling books in Canada for the week ending June 7 Read more » American poet Roger Reeves wins Griffin Poetry Prize for ‘Best Barbarian’ Read more » Author Haruki Murakami says pandemic, war in Ukraine create walls that divide people Read more » Summer is here, and the reading is amazing. Subject: Concern Over Insecure Medical Records
Body:
Dear Customer Support,

I am contacting you to report a critical issue that has arisen with our medical records. It seems unsecured medical files have been accessed externally, which is a significant concern. Based on our preliminary investigation, the issue may stem from outdated firewall rules that have not been updated recently.

To address the situation, we have already attempted to restart the server and review the logs for any signs of suspicious activity. However, the problem continues, and we have not yet fully determined the extent of the breach.

We urgently seek your help in resolving this matter and securing our medical files. Please provide us with guidance on updating our firewall settings and preventing future unauthorized access. We would be grateful if you could call us at <tel_num> to discuss the next steps.

Thank you for your swift action on this issue. We look forward to your response.

Warm regards,
[Your Name]
